stimulus:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

  • Oregon defines a wetland as a parcel of land of at least 1 acre with seasonal saturation.
  • Washington’s definition of a wetland doesn’t take parcel size into account.
  • By its own definition, Washington has over 1.5 million acres of wetlands, many in plots smaller than 1 acre.
  • By Oregon’s definition, Washington has only about 900,000 acres of wetlands.
stem: The student wants to contrast Oregon’s definition of a wetland with Washington’s. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

answerOptions.content: Oregon’s definition of a wetland—a parcel of at least 1 acre with seasonal saturation—is more restrictive than Washington’s, which doesn’t take parcel size into account.
Washington, which doesn’t take parcel size into account in defining a wetland, claims over 1.5 million acres of wetlands.
Because its definition of a wetland is different from Oregon’s, it is unclear how much wetland Washington really has.
According to Oregon’s definition of a wetland—a parcel of at least 1 acre with seasonal saturation—Washington has about 900,000 acres of wetlands.

rationale:
Choice A is the best answer. It names both states and articulates the contrast: Oregon’s size-based definition is more restrictive than Washington’s.

Choice B states one side’s definition without contrasting. Choice C says the definitions differ but doesn’t articulate how. Choice D applies Oregon’s definition to Washington without contrasting the definitions themselves.
